contents: ReviewMalignant transformation of oral lichen planus and oral lichenoidlesions: A meta-analysis of 20095 patient dataSana Maher Hasan Aghbari PhD a,1, Abdelrahman Ibrahim Abushouk MBBS b,c,|,1, Attia Attia MBBS d,1,Ahmed Elmaraezy d, Amr Menshawy d, Mohamed Shehata Ahmed e, Basma Abdelaleem Elsaadany MSc f,Eman Magdy Ahmed MSc ga Faculty of Oral and Dental Medicine, Aden University, Yemenb Faculty of Medicine, Ain Shams University, Cairo, Egyptc NovaMed Medical Research Association, Cairo, Egyptd Faculty of Medicine, Al-Azhar University, Cairo, Egypte Faculty of Medicine, Zagazig University, El-Sharkia, Egyptf Faculty of Oral and Dental Medicine, Cairo University, Cairo, Egyptg Faculty of Oral and Dental Medicine, Beni Suef University, Beni Suef, Egypta r t i c l ei n f oArticle history:Received 4 March 2017Accepted 25 March 2017Available online 5 April 2017Keywords:Oral lichen planusMalignant transformationOral cancerMeta-analysisa b s t r a c t

Objectives: For over a century, a heated debate existed over the possibility of malignant transformation oforal lichen planus (OLP). We performed this meta-analysis to evaluate the malignant potential of OLP andoral lichenoid lesions (OLL) and investigate the possible risk factors for OLP malignant transformationinto oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC).Materials and methods: We searched Medline, Scopus, and Web of Knowledge for relevant observationalstudies. Data on OLP malignant transformation were calculated as a pooled proportion (PP), using theDer-Simonian Liard method. We performed subgroup analyses by OLP diagnostic criteria, site, and clin-ical type, using Open Meta[Analyst] software. Data on possible risk factors for malignant transformationwere pooled as odds ratios (ORs), using Comprehensive Meta-Analysis software.Results: Pooling data for OLP malignant transformation from 57 studies (19,676 patients) resulted in anoverall PP of 1.1% [95% CI: 0.9%, 1.4%], while pooling data from 14 recent studies that used the WorldHealth Organization-2003 diagnostic criteria resulted in an overall-PP of 0.9% [95% CI: 0.5%, 1.3%]. Therisk of malignant transformation was higher (PP = 2.5%, 95% CI [1%, 4%]) in OLL patients (419 patients).A significant increase of malignant transformation risk was noted among smokers (OR = 2, 95% CI[1.25, 3.22]), alcoholics (OR = 3.52, 95% CI [1.54, 8.03]), and HCV-infected patients (OR = 5, 95% CI[1.56, 16.07]), compared to patients without these risk factors.Conclusion: A small subset of OLP patients (1.1%) develop OSCC; therefore, regular follow-up for thesepatients is recommended. A higher incidence of malignant transformation was found among smokers,alcoholics, and HCV-infected patients; however, these associations should be further investigated. 2017 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.Introduction

Oral Lichen Planus (OLP) is an oral subtype of Lichen Planus,affecting 1-4% of the worldwide population with a higherfrequency in middle aged and elderly women [1]. It is a chronicinflammatory disease, characterized by a T-cell mediated responseagainst epithelial basal cells, leading to basal cell degeneration andsubepithelial band like infiltration by T-lymphocytes [2,3]. Clini-cally, there are six different subtypes, classified into two groups:Non-erosive-atrophicforms[includingreticular,papularandplaquelike]anderosive-atrophicforms[includingatrophic(erythematous), erosive (ulcerative) and bullous] [4].Oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) is the most common form(90%) of oral cancer, with increasing prevalence on a global scale[5]. The first report of OLP malignant transformation was published 2017 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.Abbreviations: HCV, hepatitis C virus; OLL, oral lichenoid lesions; OLP, orallichen planus; OSCC, oral squamous cell carcinoma; PP, pooled proportion.| Corresponding author at: Faculty of Medicine, Ain Shams University, 11591,Ramsis St, Abbasia, Cairo, Egypt.E-mail address: (A.I. Abushouk).1 These authors contributed equally.

Contents lists available at ScienceDirectOral Oncologyjournal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/oraloncologyin 1910 by Hallopeau et al. [6]. Later, several reports were pub-lished to investigate the malignant potential of OLP with conflict-ing results. Several studies [7-13] have reported malignanttransformation rates for OLP that ranged between 0.07% and5.8%, while other studies [14-17] showed no malignant potentialfor OLP.The major dilemma that always confused researchers regardingthe malignant transformation of OLP was the lack of universal cri-teria for its diagnosis. In fact, Krutchkoff et al. [18] and Van DerMeij et al. [19] alerted that almost two thirds of published casereports about OLP malignant transformation were not sufficientlydocumented to be considered. Over decades, several efforts wereexerted to overcome this obstacle. In 1978, the World Health Orga-nization (WHO) developed diagnostic criteria for OLP that werebased on clinical and histopathological standards [20]. In 2003,Van der Meij and Van der Waal suggested modifying the WHO cri-teria to differentiate between OLP and other lichenoid lesions, suchas lichenoid dysplasia, which has a significantly higher risk ofmalignant transformation than OLP [21].We performed this systematic review and meta-analysis to pre-cisely evaluate the malignant potential of OLP and investigate theassociation between OLP malignant transformation and differentrelated risk factors, such as smoking, alcohol consumption, andhepatitis C infection.

MethodsWe followed the guidelines of the (MOOSE statement: meta-analysis of observational studies in epidemiology) during thepreparation of this meta-analysis [22].Literature search strategyWe searched Medline (through PubMed), Scopus, and Web ofKnowledge, using the following search strategy [Oral lichen planusOLP OR Oral lichinoid lesion OR OLL AND Malignent OR Cancer OROral squamous cell carcinoma OR OSCC]. No restrictions by publi-cation period were used. We also scanned the bibliography ofincluded studies to identify any missed studies that are relevantto our subject.Eligibility criteria and study selectionWe included full length articles of observational studies, pub-lished in the English language, in peer reviewed medical journals.All observational studies, providing dichotomous data on themalignant potential of OLP or oral lichenoid lesions (OLL), wereincluded. We excluded conference abstracts, articles published asa whole in a non-English language, case reports/series, and sec-ondary analysis articles. Two independent reviewers applied theselection criteria in two steps [Title and abstract screening, thenfull text screening] to determine the included studies and disagree-ments were solved upon the opinion of a third reviewer.Data extractionTwo independent reviewers extracted the following data fromincluded studies: (I) Characters of study design and length of fol-low up period, (II) risk of bias assessment domains, (III) character-istics of OLP lesions including the used diagnostic system criteria,site, and clinical type [Atrophic, erosive, reticular, or plaque], (IV)the incidence of malignant transformation in OLP or OLL cases,and (V) the incidence of malignant transformation in different sub-groups of OLP patients. Disagreements were resolved upon theopinion of a third reviewer.

We used the Newcastle-Ottawa scale [23] to assess the risk ofbias within the included observational studies. Using this scale,each included study is evaluated based on reporting three essentialdomains:(a) Selection of the study subjects i.e. included patients werederived from a representative population, and the exposure(OLP diagnosis) was ascertained based on predefined criteria(preferably including histological examination).(b) Comparability of groups on demographic characteristics andimportant potential confounders: which is achieved by ade-quate control of secondary risk factors, such as smoking andalcoholconsumption,aswellasadequateseparationbetween OLP and OLL cases (by excluding history of medica-tions or restorations).(c) Ascertainment of the prespecified outcome (exposure/treat-ment): based on histological assessment with a sufficientfollow up duration.Due to the design of included studies (lack of non-exposedgroup), we removed the question about the selection of the non-exposed group (Question No. 2) from the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale;therefore, the total score is out of eight, not nine [24]. Each articlewas evaluated by two independent reviewers and any disagree-ments were resolved by consenesus among both reviewers. Weassessed for the existence of publication bias, using the Egger's test[25].Data synthesis

Pooled proportion (PP) of the overall risk of malignant transfor-mation in OLP and OLL was calculated under the random effectsmodel, using Der-Simonian Liard method. We conducted a cumula-tive meta-analysis to display the trend of malignant transformationover time from 1924 to 2016. Subgroup analysis was conducted tocompare the risk of malignant transformation according to the diag-nostic criteria, clinical type, and site of OLP. We calculated a pooledodds ratio (OR), along with its 95% confidence interval (CI), to com-pare the risk of transformation according to patients' gender, smok-ing, alcohol consumption, infection with hepatitis C virus (HCV), anddiabetes mellitus. Comprehensive Meta-Analysis and Open Meta[Analyst] software were used for quantitative data synthesis.ResultsLiterature search resultsLiterature search retrieved 1200 unique records. After title/abstract screening, 197 full text articles were retrieved for furtherevaluation. Finally, 57 studies that provided data on 19,676 OLPand 419 OLL cases were eligible for inclusion in our analysis(Fig. 1). Of OLP cases, a total of 280 patients developed OSCC. Therate of malignant transformation of OLP in individual studies ran-ged from 0.3% to 14.3%. Baseline data of enrolled patients inincluded studies are illustrated in Tables 1. Table 2 displays a com-parison between demographic characteristics of OSCC patients inincluded studies and the reference profile for OSCC patients (fromthe Surveillance, Epidemiology, and End Results [SEER] database).

Risk of bias assessmentThe majority of included studies reported adequately on therepresentativenessoftheircohorts(55of57),exposureascertainment (46 of 57), and outcome assessment methods (50 of57). However, a lower number of studies reported on the length of(29 of 57) and loss during follow up (13 of 57). Control of sec-ondary risk factors and similar lesions were reported in 37 and36 studies, respectively. Details for risk of bias assessment areillustrated in Supplementary file 1.Meta-analysis for risk of malignant transformation

Single-arm meta-analysis, under the random effects model andusing Der-Simonian Liard method, resulted in an overall pooledproportion (PP) of 1.1% [95% CI: 0.9-1.4%]. There was a consider-able heterogeneity among the results of individual studies, as indi-cated by an I2 value of 60.96% and Chi-Square p < 0.001 (Fig. 2A). Acumulative analysis indicated a decreasing trend that started by5.9% transformation rate in 1924 and went down to 1.5% in1968. From 1972 to the present time, there was a relatively consis-tent rate that ranged between 0.5% and 1.1% (Fig. 2B). Consideringthe significant heterogeneity, we performed a sensitivity analysisto measure the influence of omitting single studies on the overallPP. The summary PP was consistent on removing any single studyand ranged between 1.1% and 1.2% (Supplementary file 2).We performed a subgroup analysis for the recent studies inwhich OLP was defined according to the 2003 modified WHO crite-ria [21]. The PP was 0.9% with a 95% CI ranging from 0.5% to 1.3%. Amoderate degree of heterogeneity was identified among thesestudies (I2 = 34.84%, p = 0.096) (Fig. 3A). Publication bias towardsstudies with transformation rate less than 1.1% was evident byEgger's regression test (p = 0.00038) and asymmetry of the funnelplot (Fig. 3B).Data on the overall malignant transformation of OLL into OSCCwere provided by four studies [15,59,67,68] that included 419 OLLcases. Of them, 13 cases were complicated by malignancy with arate of transformation in individual datasets, ranging between1.2% and 4.9%. The PP of malignant transformation of OLL was2.5% [95% CI: 1-4%], with no heterogeneity among pooled studies(I2 = 0%, p = 0.47).Subgroup analysis

Stratification according to the clinical type of OLP indicated thatthe rate of malignant transformation was 1.7% in erosive OLP, 1.3%in atrophic OLP, and 0.1% in the reticular type (Fig. 4). The mostcommon site of transformation was the tongue (1.05%), followedby the buccal mucosa (0.7%). The gingiva and lips had similar ratesof malignant transformation (0.6%), while the floor of the mouthdeveloped OSCC at a rate of 0.5%.Risk factors for OLP malignant transformationTwenty-eight studies provided data on rates of transformationamong males and females. Malignant transformation was moreprevalent among males than females (OR = 1.11); however, thisdifference was not statistically significant ([95% CI: 0.83, 1.48],p = 0.48) (Fig. 5A).Compared to non-smokers, smokers had a significantly higherrate of malignant transformation (OR = 2, 95% CI [1.25, 3.22],p = 0.004). Twenty-six patients among the 1268 smokers devel-oped malignancy, compared to 49 of 4387 non-smokers. Thesedata were reported by 13 homogenous studies (I2 = 0%, p = 0.68)(Fig. 5B). Similarly, pooled data from five studies showed increasedrates of malignancy among alcoholics compared to non-alcoholics(OR = 3.52, 95% CI [1.54, 8.03], p = 0.003) (Fig. 5C).Interestingly, data from two studies (561 OLP cases) displayedan increased prevalence of malignancy among HCV +ve patientsin comparison to the HCV ve group (OR = 5, 95% CI [1.56,16.07], p = 0.007) (Fig. 5D). On the other hand, there was no signif-icantdifferencebetweendiabeticandnon-diabeticpatients(OR = 1.49, 95% CI [0.48, 4.62], p = 0.49) (Fig. 5E).

DiscussionOur meta-analysis of 57 studies (20,095 OLP cases) showed that1.1% of OLP patients developed OSCC, while the rate of malignanttransformation among OLL cases was 2.5%. Selective pooling ofstudies that used the WHO 2003 diagnostic criteria showed amalignant transformation rate of 0.9%, which was slightly lowerthan the overall estimate. Although the majority of included stud-ies were heterogenous in terms of design, inclusion criteria, andlength of follow up [which was also expressed in our analysis:I2 = 60.96%], most of them reported a malignant transformationrate of nearly 1%, which is consistent with our findings.Hsue et al. Several theories were developed to explain the possible malig-nant transformation of OLP. A widely-established hypothesis is theproduction of nitric oxide (NO) by inflammatory cells within thelesion [73], which later reacts with O2 to induce the formation of8-dihydro-2C/-doxyguanosine (8-oxodG) [74]. This compound isknown to cause G-T nucleotide transversion, promoting carcino-genesis [75]. Another possible source of DNA mutation is the activ-ityofcyclo-oxygenaseIIenzymewithintheinfiltratinginflammatory cells, increasing the release of inflammatory cytoki-nes and malondialdehyde, a carcinogenic metabolite that is knownto cause DNA damage [76]. Multiple studies have reported a highproliferation rate of the basal epithelial cells within OLP lesions[77-79] and it was shown that this rate is higher in patients wholater developed OSCC than those who did not [78,80]. This phe-nomenon was explained later by the deficient function of p53apoptotic protein and overactivation of the Akt/Protein kinase-Bcell survival pathway [79,81,82].The relationship between tobacco smoking and malignanttransformation of OLP is highly debatable. Established as a carcino-gen, the main argument is whether smoking can independentlycause OSCC or just interacts with OLP to increase its malignantpotential. In the modified WHO (2003) criteria, smoking must beproperly documented to differentiate true malignant transforma-tion from cases of conventional OSCC that happened to occur inCases of oral squamous cellcarcinoma in our analysisReference statistics for oral cancer(SEER database 2012-2013 data)Lifetime risk1.1%1.1%Average age Ys (Mean +- SD)58.49 +- 8.01062

Gender N (%) [28 studies]Male69 (35.4%)70%Female126 (64.6%)30%Oral location %Tongue30.4% (12 studies)40%Buccal mucosa20.3% (12 studies)-Gingiva17.4% (12 studies)35%Mouth floor14.5% (11 studies)17%Lips17.4% (8 studies)-Others-8%N.B. The reported demographic data were derived from a subset of included studies that clearly indicated such data. The number of studies that reported on each item isclarified beside it.OLP patients [21]. According to our analysis, tobacco smokingincreases the risk of OLP malignant transformation; however,future studies should consider the points mentioned earlier.Another point of controversy is the relationship between hep-atitis C virus and OLP malignant transformation. Few studies founda high OLP malignant transformation rate in HCV endemic areasand HCV-RNA strands were detected in OLP tissues [83-85]. How-ever, OSCC cases were previously reported in HCV-infectedpatients, irrespective of OLP [84,86]. Of note, HCV can induce car-cinogenesis directly by deregulating the cell cycle [87,88] or indi-rectly by causing cirrhosis [89]. Our meta-analysis showed asignificant association between HCV infection and OLP malignanttransformation; however, this finding is based on pooling of twostudies; therefore, it needs further confirmation and validation.

Strength pointsWe performed a comprehensive, yet careful selection ofincluded studies to provide the most robust evidence about themalignant transformation of OLP. Our review is the first to investi-gate the potential of gender, smoking, alcohol consumption, andHCV as risk factors for OLP malignant transformation in a meta-analysis framework. Due to the lack of a control group from thegeneral population, we compared our data to the reference profilefor OSCC in the SEER database.Wetriedtoovercomethepossiblediagnosticconfusionbetween OLP and other lichenoid lesions in our meta-analysis by(1) performing a subgroup analysis of the studies that exclusivelyused the modified WHO 2003 or matching criteria, (2) separatingthe cases of OLP and OLL in different analytic datasets to calculatethe malignant transformation of each pathological condition inde-pendently, and (3) conducting a sensitivity analysis to ensure thatnone of the individual studies, in which diagnostic confusion couldhave occurred, significantly shifted the overall effect estimate in itsdirection.Limitations

We only included studies, published in the English language;however, there is an evidence that restricting a meta-analysis toEnglish studies does not introduce bias to its results [90]. Despiteour efforts, it may not be fully possible to discriminate OLP fromother lichenoid lesions in included studies. Moreover, the site ofOSCC was not reliably reported in the majority of included studies,opening the possibility that some OSCC cases have arisen inpreviously-healthy oral areas. There are other risk factors thatwere not investigated in this analysis due to the lack of numericaldata, such as the role of infective agents (Candida and humanpapilloma virus, in particular) and immunosuppressive drugs.Recommendations for future researchBased on the nature of the condition, the best study design toinvestigate the malignant potential of OLP would be multicentercohort studies with long follow up periods. Proper clinical andhistopathologicaldocumentationandcarefuldifferentiationbetween OLP and other lichenoid lesions should be a priority in

future studies. To provide more uniform and comparable data,we encourage investigators to use the same diagnostic criteria thatcan clearly define OLP lesions and eliminate confounding risk fac-tors, such as smoking and alcohol consumption. Further studies arerequired to investigate the relationship between OLP malignanttransformation and risk factors, such as smoking, immunosuppres-sive drugs, and infections (especially candida, HPV, and HCV).Implications for clinical practiceBased on our findings, we recommend regular follow up of OLPpatients to ensure early cancer detection. There is a debate in theliterature over the optimal number of annual visits; however,two visits per year seems adequate and technically feasible [15].Moreover, Muzio et al. found no significant value in terms of mor-tality with a higher frequency of annual visits [46]. Regarding thehealthcare professionals who should perform the follow up, Gan-dolfo et al. suggested it should be performed by general dentists toenhance their training in early cancer detection [53]. Moreover,there is no evidence in the literature that supports the need for oralmedicine specialists to perform the follow-up examinations.

The premalignant potential of OLP, the possible risk factors forsuch transformation, and the importance of follow up should becommunicated to OLP patients. Until a high-quality evidence isgenerated regarding the risk of treating OLP lesions with immuno-supressive drugs on malignant transformation, these drugs shouldbe allowed in the clinical management of OLP lesions.ConclusionOur meta-analysis supports the notion that a small subset ofOLP patients (1.1%) develop OSCC. A higher incidence of malignanttransformation was found among smokers, alcohol consumers, andHCV-infected patients; however, these associations should beinterpreted with caution. Therefore, we recommend regular followup of OLP patients to ensure early cancer detection. Future studiesshould follow a uniform criteria for OLP diagnosis and investigatethe effect of smoking, infections, and immunosupressive drugs onthe potential of OLP malignant transformation.Conflict of interestFunding sourcesNone.AcknowledgementThe authors would like to acknowledge the staff members ofthe Oral Medicine and Diagnosis Department, Faculty of Oral Med-icine, Cairo University for their continuous advice and support dur-ing conducting the study.Appendix A. Supplementary materialSupplementary data associated with this article can be found, inthe online version, at References
